title: Repository Guidelines
text: R/: core package functions grouped topic (similarity.R, multimatch.R, density.R, eye_frame.R, etc.); man/ generated roxygen, never edit directly. Tests live tests/testthat/ fixtures test_data/; mirror function names test_*.R files. Data assets: packaged objects data/, creation scripts data-raw/; vignettes styling vignettes/ (albersdown theme), site config _pkgdown.yml, rendered site docs/. Edit prose README.Rmd (README.md); pkgdown extras assets live pkgdown/.

title: Repository Guidelines
text: Install deps: R -q -e "devtools::install_deps()". Run suite: R -q -e "devtools::test()"; target file R -q -e "testthat::test_file('tests/testthat/test_similarity_emd.R')". Full check (tests, lint-style checks): R -q -e "devtools::check()". Refresh docs/NAMESPACE code changes: R -q -e "devtools::document()". Build docs site locally: R -q -e "pkgdown::build_site()". Optional: enable MultiMatch parity tests installing Python module via R -q -e "reticulate::py_install('multimatch_gaze', pip=TRUE)".

title: Repository Guidelines
text: Use <-, 2-space indents, snake_case functions/objects; S3 methods follow method.class. Favor tidyverse verbs pipelines (dplyr, purrr, %>%) eye_frame.R; keep argument order consistent across functions (data, grouping, options). Document exports roxygen2 blocks; annotate internal helpers @noRd. Update examples run quickly deterministically. Keep file object names descriptive concise (e.g., sample_density_time, multi_match); avoid long inline functions—extract helpers module.

title: Repository Guidelines
text: Place new coverage tests/testthat/test_<feature>.R; group expectations scenario seed randomness set.seed(). Prefer small, explicit fixtures (tibbles test_data/ snippets) serialized binaries; assert happy paths edge cases (empty inputs, size mismatches). Python-backed comparisons, follow existing skip_on_cran() reticulate::py_module_available() guards CI remains stable dependencies absent. Check coverage locally R -q -e "covr::package_coverage()" touching core similarity/density paths.

title: Repository Guidelines
text: Commits short, imperative, scope-focused (e.g., “Tighten template_similarity bounds”, “Add density parity tests”); batch unrelated changes separately. PRs need brief summary, command output devtools::test() devtools::check(), linked issues relevant. Note new data assets API changes. Include screenshots pkgdown previews plot outputs vignettes change. Keep branches rebased minimize conflicts.

what: How do you compare two scanpaths?
title: Comparing Scanpaths with MultiMatch
text: Start creating two fixation_group objects, convert scanpath objects, call multi_match(): Two scanpaths generated similar linear processes. metrics high, consistent known similarity two scanpaths.
code: set.seed(1)  simulate_linear <- function(n) {   fixation_group(     x = cumsum(rnorm(n, mean = 50, sd = 10)),     y = cumsum(rnorm(n, mean = 50, sd = 10)),     duration = rpois(n, lambda = 200),     onset = 1:n   ) }  fg1 <- simulate_linear(10) fg2 <- simulate_linear(12) sp1 <- scanpath(fg1) sp2 <- scanpath(fg2) multi_match(sp1, sp2, screensize = c(500, 500)) #>       mm_vector    mm_direction       mm_length     mm_position     mm_duration  #>       0.9944381       0.9824906       0.9924735       0.8774572       0.9194313  #> mm_position_emd  #>       0.9646769

what: What happens when scanpaths differ structurally?
title: Comparing Scanpaths with MultiMatch
text: Let’s compare linear scanpath zigzag pattern: Notice direction metric drops substantially — zigzag linear paths follow different angular trajectories.
code: fg_zigzag <- fixation_group(   x = cumsum(rep(50, 10)),   y = cumsum(c(50, -50, 50, -50, 50, -50, 50, -50, 50, -50)),   duration = rpois(10, lambda = 200),   onset = 1:10 )  sp_linear <- scanpath(simulate_linear(10)) sp_zigzag <- scanpath(fg_zigzag) multi_match(sp_linear, sp_zigzag, screensize = c(500, 500)) #>       mm_vector    mm_direction       mm_length     mm_position     mm_duration  #>       0.9914539       0.9671332       0.9912823       0.6570499       0.9350000  #> mm_position_emd  #>       0.6931491

title: Measuring Similarity Across Repeated Viewings
text: Let’s simulate small dataset: 2 participants view 3 images encoding retrieval. Compute density maps participant-phase-image combination:
code: set.seed(42)  gen_fixations <- function(imname, phase, participant) {   nfix <- sample(3:10, 1)   data.frame(     x = runif(nfix, 0, 100), y = runif(nfix, 0, 100),     onset = cumsum(runif(nfix, 20, 80)),     duration = runif(nfix, 80, 300),     image = imname, phase = phase, participant = participant   ) }  df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(c("s1", "s2"), function(s) {   do.call(rbind, lapply(c("encoding", "retrieval"), function(p) {     do.call(rbind, lapply(paste0("img", 1:3), function(img) {       gen_fixations(img, p, s)     }))   })) }))  eyetab <- eye_table("x", "y", "duration", "onset",                     groupvar = c("participant", "phase", "image"),                     data = df) eyedens <- density_by(eyetab,                       groups = c("phase", "image", "participant"),                       sigma = 50,                       xbounds = c(0, 100), ybounds = c(0, 100))

title: Measuring Similarity Across Repeated Viewings
text: repetitive_similarity() takes density table condition variable (phase) defines repeated viewings: result contains two key columns: repsim — within-stimulus similarity: consistently participants looked locations image across conditions othersim — cross-stimulus similarity: baseline similarity different images viewed different conditions
code: rep_sim <- repetitive_similarity(eyedens,                                  condition_var = "phase",                                  method = "pearson") rep_sim #> # A tibble: 12 × 7 #>    phase     image participant fixgroup            density    repsim othersim #>    <chr>     <chr> <chr>       <list>              <list>      <dbl>    <dbl> #>  1 encoding  img1  s1          <fxtn_grp [3 × 6]>  <ey_dnsty> 0.304     0.367 #>  2 encoding  img1  s2          <fxtn_grp [9 × 6]>  <ey_dnsty> 0.377     0.626 #>  3 encoding  img2  s1          <fxtn_grp [6 × 6]>  <ey_dnsty> 0.423     0.506 #>  4 encoding  img2  s2          <fxtn_grp [6 × 6]>  <ey_dnsty> 0.461     0.694 #>  5 encoding  img3  s1          <fxtn_grp [10 × 6]> <ey_dnsty> 0.204     0.243 #>  6 encoding  img3  s2          <fxtn_grp [3 × 6]>  <ey_dnsty> 0.0566    0.388 #>  7 retrieval img1  s1          <fxtn_grp [9 × 6]>  <ey_dnsty> 0.688     0.582 #>  8 retrieval img1  s2          <fxtn_grp [5 × 6]>  <ey_dnsty> 0.531     0.408 #>  9 retrieval img2  s1          <fxtn_grp [5 × 6]>  <ey_dnsty> 0.534     0.565 #> 10 retrieval img2  s2          <fxtn_grp [3 × 6]>  <ey_dnsty> 0.341     0.389 #> 11 retrieval img3  s1          <fxtn_grp [7 × 6]>  <ey_dnsty> 0.538     0.587 #> 12 retrieval img3  s2          <fxtn_grp [5 × 6]>  <ey_dnsty> 0.312     0.293

title: Measuring Similarity Across Repeated Viewings
text: Within-stimulus similarity (repsim). Higher values indicate participants fixated similar locations viewing image across encoding retrieval. Cross-stimulus similarity (othersim). represents baseline: similar gaze patterns comparing different images across conditions. random data, values near zero. real experiment, expect repsim higher othersim participants show stimulus-specific eye-movement reinstatement. difference (repsim - othersim) gives corrected measure reinstatement controls general gaze tendencies.
code: ggplot(rep_sim, aes(x = image, y = repsim)) +   geom_col(fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.7) +   labs(x = "Stimulus", y = "Repetitive Similarity",        title = "Same image across conditions") +   theme_minimal() ggplot(rep_sim, aes(x = image, y = othersim)) +   geom_col(fill = "coral", alpha = 0.7) +   labs(x = "Stimulus", y = "Other Similarity",        title = "Different images across conditions") +   theme_minimal()

what: How do you visualize fixation density?
title: Comparing Eye-Movement Patterns
text: Individual points can hard interpret. Density maps show fixations cluster. plot() method supports several display styles: Three density visualizations: contour, raster, filled contour. bandwidth parameter controls smoothing level. Higher values blur fine detail emphasize broad patterns: Effect bandwidth: narrow (20), medium (60), wide (100).
code: p1 <- plot(fg, typ = "contour", xlim = c(-10, 110), ylim = c(-10, 110), bandwidth = 35) p2 <- plot(fg, typ = "raster", xlim = c(-10, 110), ylim = c(-10, 110), bandwidth = 35) p3 <- plot(fg, typ = "filled_contour", xlim = c(-10, 110), ylim = c(-10, 110), bandwidth = 35) p1 + p2 + p3 p1 <- plot(fg, typ = "filled_contour", xlim = c(-10, 110), ylim = c(-10, 110), bandwidth = 20) p2 <- plot(fg, typ = "filled_contour", xlim = c(-10, 110), ylim = c(-10, 110), bandwidth = 60) p3 <- plot(fg, typ = "filled_contour", xlim = c(-10, 110), ylim = c(-10, 110), bandwidth = 100) p1 + p2 + p3

what: How do you compare two fixation patterns?
title: Comparing Eye-Movement Patterns
text: quantify similar two fixation patterns , convert eye_density maps call similarity(). create two patterns share roughly half fixation locations: Two fixation patterns sharing roughly half locations. Now convert density maps compute similarity: default metric Pearson correlation. Several alternatives available:
code: set.seed(123) x_shared <- runif(12, 0, 100) y_shared <- runif(12, 0, 100)  fg1 <- fixation_group(   x = c(x_shared, runif(13, 0, 100)),   y = c(y_shared, runif(13, 0, 100)),   onset = cumsum(runif(25, 0, 100)),   duration = runif(25, 50, 300) )  fg2 <- fixation_group(   x = c(x_shared, runif(13, 0, 100)),   y = c(y_shared, runif(13, 0, 100)),   onset = cumsum(runif(25, 0, 100)),   duration = runif(25, 50, 300) ) ed1 <- eye_density(fg1, sigma = 50, xbounds = c(0, 100), ybounds = c(0, 100)) ed2 <- eye_density(fg2, sigma = 50, xbounds = c(0, 100), ybounds = c(0, 100)) similarity(ed1, ed2) #> [1] 0.7760392 methods <- c("pearson", "spearman", "fisherz", "cosine", "l1", "jaccard", "dcov") results <- sapply(methods, function(m) similarity(ed1, ed2, method = m)) data.frame(method = methods, similarity = round(unlist(results), 4)) #>            method similarity #> pearson   pearson     0.7760 #> spearman spearman     0.7714 #> fisherz   fisherz     1.0353 #> cosine     cosine     0.9934 #> l1             l1     0.9484 #> jaccard   jaccard     0.9869 #> dcov         dcov     0.7293

title: Comparing Eye-Movement Patterns
text: template_similarity() compares retrieval density map matched encoding density map. estimates baseline permuting image labels reports corrected difference: result includes three key columns: eye_sim — raw similarity matched pairs perm_sim — average similarity across non-matching pairs (baseline) eye_sim_diff — corrected score (raw minus baseline) Since data random, true reinstatement: Distribution raw, permuted, corrected similarity scores. expected, corrected similarity centered near zero. note units. method = "fisherz", similarity values Fisher z scores (atanh Pearson r). Convert back correlations tanh() desired. method = "pearson" "spearman", values correlations directly. note permutations. permutations argument upper bound. fewer non-matching candidates available, eyesim uses exhaustively.
code: set.seed(1234) enc_dens <- eyedens %>% filter(phase == "encoding") ret_dens <- eyedens %>% filter(phase == "retrieval")  simres <- template_similarity(enc_dens, ret_dens,                               match_on = "image",                               method = "fisherz",                               permutations = 50) t.test(simres$eye_sim_diff) #>  #>  One Sample t-test #>  #> data:  simres$eye_sim_diff #> t = 0.78502, df = 59, p-value = 0.4356 #> alternative hypothesis: true mean is not equal to 0 #> 95 percent confidence interval: #>  -0.1021582  0.2340630 #> sample estimates: #>  mean of x  #> 0.06595242

text: Choosing single bandwidth somewhat arbitrary. can compute density multiple scales passing vector sigma values: Multiscale analysis provides robust similarity estimate averaging across spatial resolutions.
code: eyedens_multi <- density_by(eyetab,                             groups = c("phase", "image", "participant"),                             sigma = c(25, 50, 100),                             xbounds = c(0, 100), ybounds = c(0, 100))  enc_multi <- eyedens_multi %>% filter(phase == "encoding") ret_multi <- eyedens_multi %>% filter(phase == "retrieval")  simres_multi <- template_similarity(enc_multi, ret_multi,                                     match_on = "image",                                     method = "fisherz",                                     permutations = 50)  cat("Single-scale mean:", round(mean(simres$eye_sim_diff), 4), "\n") #> Single-scale mean: 0.066 cat("Multiscale mean:  ", round(mean(simres_multi$eye_sim_diff), 4), "\n") #> Multiscale mean:   0.0342

title: Latent Transforms for Template Similarity
text: template_similarity() compares density maps raw screen space. encoding retrieval differ systematic transform — different screen sizes, calibration drift sessions, participant-specific domain shifts, contraction/distortion phases — raw correlation can underestimate true reinstatement. eyesim supports two families transform hooks similarity computed: latent transforms PCA, CORAL, CCA geometric transforms contraction affine warps add template_similarity() two arguments; core API stays unchanged.

title: Latent Transforms for Template Similarity
text: Suppose retrieval contracted shifted version encoding. better match geometric transform latent covariance method, use contract_transform() running example: Now create source set whose densities shifted expanded best alignment global contraction plus translation:
code: set.seed(901)  make_gaussian_density <- function(mean = c(0, 0), cov = diag(c(0.2, 0.2)),                                   x = seq(-2, 2, length.out = 25),                                   y = seq(-2, 2, length.out = 25)) {   coords <- as.matrix(expand.grid(x = x, y = y))   inv_cov <- solve(cov)   centered <- sweep(coords, 2, mean, FUN = "-")   expo <- rowSums((centered %*% inv_cov) * centered)   z <- exp(-0.5 * expo)   z <- matrix(z, nrow = length(x), ncol = length(y))   z <- z / sum(z)    structure(     list(z = z, x = x, y = y, sigma = 1),     class = c("density", "eye_density")   ) }  ref_means <- replicate(12, runif(2, -0.8, 0.8), simplify = FALSE) ref_cov <- matrix(c(0.18, 0.02, 0.02, 0.12), nrow = 2) scale_true <- 0.72 shift_true <- c(0.18, -0.12) ref_tab <- tibble(   id = seq_along(ref_means),   density = lapply(ref_means, function(mu) {     make_gaussian_density(mean = mu, cov = ref_cov)   }) ) source_tab <- tibble(   id = seq_along(ref_means),   density = lapply(ref_means, function(mu_ref) {     mu_src <- as.numeric((mu_ref - shift_true) / scale_true)     cov_src <- ref_cov / (scale_true^2)     make_gaussian_density(mean = mu_src, cov = cov_src)   }) )

text: Without transform, contraction distortion reduces similarity: Now fit contract_transform() matched rows compare -sample result: Paired comparison raw vs. contract-transformed similarity item. geometric transform recovers higher similarity distortion global contraction plus translation. transform recovers substantially higher similarity simulated distortion matches model. example intentionally fit evaluated rows, shows transform can recover, leakage-free effect estimate.
code: raw <- template_similarity(ref_tab, source_tab,                            match_on = "id",                            permutations = 0,                            method = "cosine") raw_mean <- mean(raw$eye_sim) stopifnot(is.finite(raw_mean), nrow(raw) == nrow(ref_tab)) cat("Raw mean similarity:", round(raw_mean, 3), "\n") #> Raw mean similarity: 0.809 contract_res <- template_similarity(   ref_tab, source_tab,   match_on = "id",   permutations = 0,   method = "cosine",   similarity_transform = contract_transform,   similarity_transform_args = list(shrink = 1e-6) ) contract_mean <- mean(contract_res$eye_sim) stopifnot(   is.finite(contract_mean),   nrow(contract_res) == nrow(ref_tab),   contract_mean > raw_mean ) cat("Contract mean similarity:", round(contract_mean, 3), "\n") #> Contract mean similarity: 0.999

code: make_density_vec <- function(vec) {   structure(     list(       z = matrix(vec, nrow = 2, ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE),       x = 1:2,       y = 1:2,       sigma = 50     ),     class = c("density", "eye_density")   ) }  set.seed(123) n_coral <- 80 base_mat <- matrix(rnorm(n_coral * 4), nrow = n_coral, ncol = 4) scale_mat <- diag(c(2, 0.6, 1.7, 0.8)) source_mat <- base_mat %*% scale_mat  coral_ref_tab <- tibble(   id = seq_len(n_coral),   density = lapply(seq_len(n_coral), function(i) make_density_vec(base_mat[i, ])) ) coral_source_tab <- tibble(   id = seq_len(n_coral),   density = lapply(seq_len(n_coral), function(i) make_density_vec(source_mat[i, ])) ) raw_coral <- template_similarity(   coral_ref_tab,   coral_source_tab,   match_on = "id",   permutations = 0,   method = "cosine" )  coral_positive <- template_similarity(   coral_ref_tab,   coral_source_tab,   match_on = "id",   permutations = 0,   method = "cosine",   similarity_transform = coral_transform,   similarity_transform_args = list(comps = 4, shrink = 1e-6) )  raw_coral_mean <- mean(raw_coral$eye_sim) coral_positive_mean <- mean(coral_positive$eye_sim)  stopifnot(   is.finite(raw_coral_mean),   is.finite(coral_positive_mean),   coral_positive_mean > raw_coral_mean )  cat("Raw CORAL toy mean similarity:", round(raw_coral_mean, 3), "\n") #> Raw CORAL toy mean similarity: 0.928 cat("CORAL toy mean similarity:", round(coral_positive_mean, 3), "\n") #> CORAL toy mean similarity: 0.996

code: contract_cv <- template_similarity_cv(   ref_tab,   source_tab,   match_on = "id",   split_on = "id",   n_folds = 4,   permutations = 0,   method = "cosine",   similarity_transform = contract_transform,   similarity_transform_args = list(shrink = 1e-6) )  contract_cv_mean <- mean(contract_cv$eye_sim) cv_info <- attr(contract_cv, "similarity_cv")  stopifnot(   is.finite(contract_cv_mean),   nrow(contract_cv) == nrow(ref_tab),   !is.null(cv_info),   length(cv_info$folds) == 4,   contract_cv_mean > raw_mean )  cat("Held-out contract mean similarity:", round(contract_cv_mean, 3), "\n") #> Held-out contract mean similarity: 0.999 template_similarity_cv(   ref_tab,   source_tab,   match_on = "scene_id",   split_on = c("participant", "scene_id"),   similarity_transform = contract_transform,   similarity_transform_args = list(fit_by = "participant"),   fit_source_filter = function(tab) tab$phase == "scene",   eval_source_filter = function(tab) tab$phase == "delay",   method = "cosine" )

title: Compute MultiMatch Metrics for Scanpath Similarity — multi_match

what: Suggest Kernel Bandwidth for Density Estimation — suggest_sigma
title: Suggest Kernel Bandwidth for Density Estimation — suggest_sigma
text: Estimates reasonable kernel bandwidth (sigma) fixation density maps using 2D variant Silverman's rule thumb, adapted eye-tracking data. estimate accounts spatial spread fixations sample size.

title: Suggest Kernel Bandwidth for Density Estimation — suggest_sigma
code: suggest_sigma(x, y = NULL, xbounds = NULL, ybounds = NULL)

title: Suggest Kernel Bandwidth for Density Estimation — suggest_sigma
text: x fixation_group object, numeric vector x-coordinates. y numeric vector y-coordinates (used x fixation_group). xbounds Optional numeric vector length 2 x-axis display bounds. Used scale estimate relative display size. ybounds Optional numeric vector length 2 y-axis display bounds. Used scale estimate relative display size.

title: Suggest Kernel Bandwidth for Density Estimation — suggest_sigma
text: single numeric value representing suggested sigma   (kernel standard deviation coordinate units).

title: Suggest Kernel Bandwidth for Density Estimation — suggest_sigma
text: function uses 2D Silverman rule: \(\sigma = n^{-1/6} \cdot \sqrt{(IQR_x^2 + IQR_y^2)/2} / 1.349\). display bounds provided, result clamped 1% 15% mean display dimension avoid extreme values.

title: Suggest Kernel Bandwidth for Density Estimation — suggest_sigma
code: fg <- fixation_group(x = runif(30, 0, 1280), y = runif(30, 0, 1024),                      onset = cumsum(rep(200, 30)), duration = rep(200, 30)) suggest_sigma(fg, xbounds = c(0, 1280), ybounds = c(0, 1024)) #> [1] 172.8

title: template_similarity — template_similarity
text: Compute similarity density map source_tab matching ("template") density map ref_tab.

title: template_similarity — template_similarity
code: template_similarity(   ref_tab,   source_tab,   match_on,   permute_on = NULL,   refvar = "density",   sourcevar = "density",   method = c("spearman", "pearson", "fisherz", "cosine", "l1", "jaccard", "dcov", "emd"),   permutations = 10,   multiscale_aggregation = "mean",   similarity_transform = NULL,   similarity_transform_args = list(),   ... )

title: template_similarity — template_similarity
text: ref_tab data frame tibble containing reference density maps. source_tab data frame tibble containing source density maps. match_on character string representing variable used match density maps ref_tab source_tab. permute_on character string representing variable used stratify permutations (default NULL). refvar character string representing name variable containing density maps reference table (default "density"). sourcevar character string representing name variable containing density maps source table (default "density"). method character string specifying similarity method use. Possible values "spearman", "pearson", "fisherz", "cosine", "l1", "jaccard", "dcov" (default "spearman"). permutations numeric value specifying number permutations baseline map (default 10). multiscale_aggregation density maps multiscale (.e., `eye_density_multiscale` objects), specifies aggregate similarities different scales. Options: "mean" (default, returns average similarity across scales), "none" (returns list vector similarities, one per scale, within result columns). See `similarity.eye_density_multiscale`. similarity_transform Optional preprocessing hook applied similarity computed. function accepts (ref_tab, source_tab, match_on, refvar, sourcevar) returns list updated tables/column names. See `latent_pca_transform`, `coral_transform`, `cca_transform`. similarity_transform_args Named list extra arguments passed `similarity_transform`. ... Extra arguments pass `similarity` function.

title: template_similarity — template_similarity
text: data frame tibble containing source table additional columns similarity scores permutation results.

title: template_similarity — template_similarity
text: Permutation baseline exhaustive behavior: set permutation candidates determined permute_on. permute_on provided, candidates restricted within stratum (e.g., within-participant); otherwise reference items candidates. permutations less number available non-matching candidates, random subset size drawn (without replacement) trial. Internally, sampling performed fixed future seed aid reproducibility. permutations greater equal number available non-matching candidates, procedure uses candidates (excluding true match). words, permutation baseline exhaustive possible. small-N designs, can set permutations large number trigger exhaustive behavior. example, 3 images per participant permute_on = participant, 2 non-matching candidates per trial; permutations >= 2 result using . Returned columns units: eye_sim: observed similarity matched pair, scale method. perm_sim: mean similarity across permuted non-matching pairs (scale eye_sim). eye_sim_diff: eye_sim - perm_sim. Units match method. Notes method interpretation: method = "fisherz", values Fisher z (atanh Pearson r). Convert back r via tanh(z) reporting correlation scale. method = "pearson" "spearman", values correlations (roughly [-1, 1]). methods (e.g., "emd", "cosine") produce scores respective scales.

See `similarity.eye_density_multiscale`. similarity_transform Optional preprocessing hook applied similarity computed. function accepts (ref_tab, source_tab, match_on, refvar, sourcevar) returns list updated tables/column names.
